key_insight: Mg transmutation and intrinsic defects in 3C-SiC reduce thermal conductivity in a strongly configuration-dependent way, with V_C and Mg_TC scattering phonons much more strongly than isolated Mg_Si.
engineering_relevance: The MLIP4SiC-Mg framework enables atomistically resolved prediction of heat transport in irradiated SiC, supporting design and lifetime assessment of nuclear ceramics under fusion and fission conditions.
method: Trained a machine-learning interatomic potential on a large DFT dataset for pristine, intrinsic-defect, Mg-defect, and Mg-complex configurations.
Combined Green-Kubo molecular dynamics with force-error correction to compute thermal conductivity accurately in large defective supercells.
Applied a resistance-based treatment to quantify dilute-defect scattering and compare isolated defects with clustered defect complexes.
evidence_signal: The model reproduces DFT energies, forces, equation-of-state behavior, phonon dispersions, and lattice thermal conductivities, and predicts pristine 3C-SiC thermal conductivity of 421 W/(mK) at 300 K in agreement with experiment.
watch_item: Defect-induced resistance is not strictly linear with concentration, so dilute-defect extrapolations and defect-cluster interactions must be handled carefully.
paper_title: Thermal Transport in SiC with Intrinsic Defects and Mg Transmutation Products
